title: MD
biography: Eric Kandel is an Austrian-American neuroscientist who received the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 2000 for discoveries concerning signal transduction in the nervous system and the molecular basis of memory. Using the sea slug Aplysia, he demonstrated how synaptic connections are modified during learning.
aiSummary: Eric Kandel is a neuroscience specialist with an H-index of 180 at Columbia University. Nobel Prize 2000 for discoveries on the molecular basis of memory. Based in New York, United States.
education: New York University School of Medicine

Subregion- and Cell Type–Restricted Gene Knockout in Mouse Brain
Genetic Demonstration of a Role for PKA in the Late Phase of LTP and in Hippocampus-Based Long-Term Memory
Recombinant BDNF Rescues Deficits in Basal Synaptic Transmission and Hippocampal LTP in BDNF Knockout Mice
Impaired Long-Term Potentiation, Spatial Learning, and Hippocampal Development in fyn Mutant Mice
The Molecular and Systems Biology of Memory
The long and the short of long–term memory—a molecular framework

awards: Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine (2000)

timeline.title: Joined New York University faculty
timeline.description: Began groundbreaking studies on Aplysia californica to understand memory

timeline.title: Moved to Columbia University
timeline.description: Established laboratory at Columbia that became a world center for neuroscience

timeline.title: Became HHMI Investigator
timeline.description: Named a Howard Hughes Medical Institute investigator

timeline.title: Nobel Prize for work on memory
timeline.description: Awarded Nobel Prize for discovering how synaptic connections change during learning and memory formation
